data.title: Why Grains And Oilseed Options Are Trending Shorter Term
data.description:
By Emily Balsamo and Eunice Son

At a Glance

  • Around 40% of grains and oilseed options volume is now traded at 30 days-to-expiry or less.
  • Weekly options can offer greater flexibility and more granular hedging opportunities, complementing standard, long-dated American options.

Agricultural derivatives are witnessing a structural shift in how participants manage risk, express a view and trade around key events in recent years. At the heart of this transformation is the growing popularity of weekly and short-dated options, as well as the increased prevalence of standard options traded with shorter days-to-expiry (DTE).

Short-Dated Options: A Structural Shift

While Friday Weekly options have long provided shorter-term risk management opportunities, Weekday options on Corn, Soybean, Soybean Meal, Soybean Oil and Wheat futures that expire on Mondays, Tuesdays, Wednesdays and Thursdays were launched in February 2025, offering enhanced flexibility. In the grains and oilseed options complex, these shorter-term products have not only gained traction but now account for a significant share of overall trading volume. In Q2 2025, nearly 14% of grains and oilseed options volume was attributable to short-dated options, up from about 5% in the same quarter of 2020.

Short-Dated New Crop options (SDNCOs) have also gained a strong foothold in the grains and oilseed options space. SDNCOs, which are monthly options listed during the planting season and settling to November Soybean or December Corn futures, have increasingly become instruments of choice for market participants seeking precise, short-term risk management tools tailored to specific crop cycles and pricing windows. Along with New Crop Weekly options, SDNCOs allow more precise risk management tied to planting and harvest, USDA reports and weather events for new crop exposure. The popularity of SDNCOs underscores how options markets are evolving, not just in terms of volume but also in functionality and risk management.

Days to Expiration Trend Down

While options initially listed at short tenors have driven significant growth in grains and oilseed options volume in recent years, the complex has also seen a trend toward trading standard long-dated options at lower average DTE.

While Friday Weekly options on grains and oilseed futures are listed for only three consecutive weeks out (and Monday through Thursday options for only two), standard American options on grains and oilseed futures are listed for up to two years out. Although the listing cycles of standard American options allow for long-term exposure, there is an increase in trading activity with fewer days to expiration for these products as well, again speaking to the needs of market participants to manage short-term risk.

In the first half of 2025, options volume at 30 DTE or less made up over 40% of total volume. Within that range, the volume of options expiring between seven and 30 days is climbing, with shorter DTE trading - such as 0 day, 1 day, or 2-7 DTE volume - taking up a growing portion of the total volume in grains and oilseed options products. In 2020, nearly 27% of grains and oilseed options volume was traded at 90 or more DTE; in 2025, that share dropped to less than 21%, while the share of options trading at 0 DTE has nearly doubled, from 1.25% to 2.29% in that time.

Since 2020, more than 36% of volume traded in options with an underlying Corn future has occurred at 30 DTE or less. Exceeding Corn’s share, 40% of total Soybean and Soybean Oil options volume has been traded at 30 DTE or less over that same period, with Chicago Wheat’s share exceeding 46%. The relatively high share of low-DTE volume within the Chicago Wheat options complex may reflect the needs of market participants to hedge supply-side geopolitical risk, while Soybean Oil may carry demand-side, short-term overnight risk due to changes in renewable fuel policy.

Offerings Respond to New Risk Management Needs

In an environment where supply chains are vulnerable to weather shocks, geopolitics and shifting trade policies, short-dated options and lower DTE strategies allow users to hedge around specific events. The rising popularity of weekly and short-dated options, coupled with the rising trend of trading longer-dated options at a lower average DTE, underscores a broader shift in market behavior.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hims & Hers Health's Transformation Into A Precision Health Platform
data.description:
Investors still see Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) as a telemedicine shop. In reality, it’s evolving into a full-stack, data-driven precision health platform with software-like economics, predictable recurring revenue, and increasing operating leverage. What some dismiss as product mix noise or Glucagon-like Peptide 1 (GLP-1) volatility is, in fact, strategic infrastructure building. Hims & Hers' latest earnings highlight a 73% year-over-year revenue surge driven by rapid subscriber growth, rising ARPU from personalized multi-condition plans, and strong operating leverage that’s scaling profit alongside top-line expansion.

From Product Sales to the Personalization Flywheel

Their central thesis is not a product, though, but a flywheel. Hims & Hers is winning demand through consumer UX such as apps, converting it into a provider-centric customized treatment programs, and maintaining through data, form-factor innovation, and owned fulfillment.

Sequential Q1 2025 to Q2 revenue drop from $586 million to $545 million was mainly a result of normalization in online revenue after the front-loaded boost from GLP-1 weight-loss drug launches in late 2024 into early 2025. Seasonal patterns and a stricter regulatory climate for compounded semaglutide also tempered new starts modestly. That’s not really the business slowing down, but rather, the platform finding its natural growth rhythm. Revenues in spite of decline, climbed 73% year over year, driven by 31% growth in subscribers and a ~30% increase in monetization per user.

The drop of Quarterly Online Revenue per Average Subscriber from $84 in Q1 2025 to $74 during Q2 constitutes mix shift, not decreasing engagement. In Q1, the ARPU surged short-term as a result of excellent initial adoption of weight-loss programs and front-loaded Rx fills, beefing up short-term revenue across subscribers. In Q2, that surge evened out as more customers transitioned into typical maintenance regimes of lower monthly expenses, and newer subscriber additions, though robust, tipped to earlier-stage customers still in the process of developing spend. To me, the drop in ARPU’s not to be feared, but a shift to flatter, steadier revenue.

Additionally, the personalized product imparts sustainability to the model. Already, nearly 1.5 million members are registered for personalized therapy plans, and individual-plan subscribers for management of multiple ailments surpassed 500, up about 170% year-over-year. The shift is visually clear in subscriber mix, with personalized plans surging to 1.5 million in Q2 2025, an 89% year-over-year increase, while non-personalized subscriptions have steadily declined. In my view, numbers like this don’t just happen, they come from owning the customer relationship and the tools that keep it strong.

In addition to convenience, the company is building features around its app, which minimize friction, from customized titration calendars to behavior trackers. It all engenders further engagement and provides structured data upon which providers can act. It's not commodity ecommerce, it's a data flywheel that refines routes to care over time and facilitates higher ARPU.

GLP-1 Engagement as the Retention Engine

Weight control is a paradigm case. In three months after starting an individualised programme incorporating a GLP-1, patients consult a professional six times on average, and 90% of patients download the app, which they use approximately 1.5 times per week.

Global GLP-1 agonist anti-obesity drugs market size will grow from $13.8 billion in 2024 to 2030, growing at a 18.5% CAGR, being predominantly driven by sheer sky rocketing demands of semaglutide, tirzepatide, and upcoming next-generation drugs.

In-house reported company-quoted assessment of patients who were on bespoke GLP-1 therapy for six months indicated average 10.3% weight reduction with tolerable side-effect profile and 25% discontinuation during the first six months. Such levels of engagement tend to translate into the high adherence and low churn in the long run, further solidifying platform's long-term retention leadership.

The second vector is international expansion. Hims & Hers' purchase of ZAVA Global pulls the company further into the UK, Germany, France, and Ireland, where management has already signaled a minimum of $50 million of revenue contribution at flat to positive adjusted EBITDA margin by year-end and a trajectory to a multi-hundred million European revenue opportunity in the future. Early international success counts because the platform approach scales most optimally in large, diffuse markets with limited in-person access to care.

The third is the vector of infrastructure. What appears to be near-term margin pressure is verticalization strategy. An in-house outsourcing facility of $503 billion in sterile injectables has been bought, and manufacturing and laboratory assets have been acquired. Property, equipment, and software have gone up about three times year to date to $205 million with automation and pharmacy capabilities, and capital expenditure and software investment meaningfully grew significantly in the first part of the year. These old-fashioned groundwork investments will ramp up SKU personalization, build throughput faster, and reduce per-unit cost as volume scales.

From Telehealth to Tech Valuation: Why HIMS Trades Like Software

HIMS' investors are basically investing in software. The equation for growth is ARPU, retention, and penetration of cross sells. Its monthly online revenue per subscriber and the subscribers are reported and together form an estimate of the subscription.

With this perspective, the company already has a business running at an implied, subscriber based revenue run rate of over $2 billion, with tailwinds in international expansion, lab based personalization, and other forms factors in hormonal health and longevity medicine. Of particular significance here is the trend to multi condition treatment plans, as this structurally grows the cross sell canvas and diminishes the risk of churn as the platform addresses more than one issue per end point customer. I think the market is still treating HIMS like a basic telehealth brand, but the numbers already tell a much bigger story.

The Rule of 40 is educational in terms of measuring efficiency. With Q2's 73% revenue growth and around 15% adjusted EBITDA margin, Hims & Hers reports a Rule of 40 score around 88. That's a software-class profile for a health consumer company still recovering gross-margin headwinds from weight loss mix, which accords with the argument that the model underlying is scaling on quality, not market intensity alone.

Hims & Hers still operates like a transactional telemedicine store but has a business profile more akin to a scaled subscription business with software-like retention and monetization breadth. With the 2026 revenue of $2.8 billion and a 5–6x EV/revenue multiple, typical of high-growth, high-profile consumer health platforms, translate to $14–$16.8 billion enterprise value, about 40% above EV today.

Risks: Regulatory, Competitive, and Execution Hurdles for Hims & Hers

Three segments lead the risk profile. First, regulatory shifts, and in particular, those surrounding the compounded GLP-1s, can redefine product mix and composition and precipitate short-term revenue and gross-margin volatility. Second, competition is accruing momentum with the incumbents and large-tech new players clustering into the digital-first health and weight-loss segment; share maintenance will necessitate ongoing investment in brand equity, fulfillment infrastructure, and caregiver-workflow-based information drives.

Finally, execution risk associated with scaling automation, sterile capacity, and foreign expansion is significant. High capex and inventory associated with those projects mute free cash flow in the near term, and weakness in subscriber growth or diminished adoption in multi-condition plans would slow the multiple expansion ingrained in the bull case. Liquidity strength, low-cost debt, and operating leverage reduce the risks but are still the fulcrum of the valuation.

Final Take

Hims & Hers' true strength lies not in GLP-1 buzz or headline growth, but interface control of care. In my view, the real upside here isn’t the GLP-1 buzz, it’s the way a sticky, data-rich model compounds over time. With ownership of the engagement loop via an app, personalized therapy paths, and fulfillment infrastructure, the company is building a proprietary dataset on tracked consumer health behavior at scale.

data.title: HighPeak Energy: Debt Covenants Relaxed
data.description:
HighPeak Energy (NASDAQ:HPK) recently announced a relaxation of the debt covenants as the company continues its returns to the good graces of the debt market. Remember the past where the debt ratio headed past 3 and the debt market was not happy at all? Here was the previous solution. But again, time was running out on that idea. The company had really left the banking industry and debt market while refinancing with essentially "private equity" money. That's expensive but was necessary as there was likely nowhere else to go. The next few articles covered how management began to "follow the debt market and stock market rules." That's gradually bringing back some financial flexibility that was lacking. Obviously more needs to be done. Giving the company more time past September 2026 is a start back to a more traditional debt structure.

The last article noted that production was essentially flat as the company adopted a much more traditional strategy that included a dividend and "living within means." This company essentially borrowed to get to roughly 50,000 BOED fast when the debt market "issued a speeding ticket" and the company could no longer issue bonds while losing its bank as well.

Offsetting this may be the efficiencies gained by production at the level of roughly 50,000 BOED. Combine that with the struggling companies I follow (because of the debt market changes) that were avoided here by getting that production to a decent level. A company like Ring Energy (REI) which just got to the 20,000 BOED (with the latest acquisition), has of course announced cost decreases attributed to a number of reasons. Another one is Yangarra Resources (OTCPK:YGRAF) which is struggling to stay above 10,000 BOED. That production level allowed the "Oil Field Services" group to come into being and save money. HighPeak Energy likely saw those struggles and more ahead of time. Management likely decided that 50,000 BOED was an ideal level that avoided some of the costs at lessor levels that companies like Ring Energy and Yangarra Resources have had until they got production up to a cost savings level.

The above is a big improvement over what the past few articles have covered. However, it does appear that management has accepted the new market and debt rules compared to when it bought and sold companies in the past. What will likely come next in the future is a return to the bond market at some point.

Both Mr. Market and the debt market will be wary. But management does not have to do anything spectacular to keep those worries declining. It's all up to management now.

There's also a hedging strategy in place that likely helps progress as well.

Stock Price

Additionally, the stock price may be reacting to the political uncertainty combined with the recent debt issues.

Obviously, the fast growth story has probably been interrupted longer than Mr. Market would have tolerance for. But it's very likely that the company is in reasonable financial condition with a conservative debt ratio.

Understand that the fast growth meant steeper production declines because the first-year decline curve will dominate during a period of fast growth. Maintenance capital will decline just by holding production reasonably steady as the company has done. Then older production with lower decline rates becomes more significant.

Fortunately, this company was one of the first to get to Howard County before some big names got there and pushed up acreage costs. That could mean that decent cash flow is "around the corner," or at least increasing cash flow as the company continues to rationalize operations.

There may be additional help in that Howard County acreage usually has some very low breakeven points. But commodity prices seem to be responding to the ongoing political uncertainty by weakening as a lot of industry peers pull back until more certainty is restored. That may benefit a low breakeven point place like this because the company can complete cheaper wells profitably to build in some lower than usual costs before an expected industry recovery.

Earnings

The EBITDAX calculation is particularly enlightening when growth slows.

Two things are influencing this calculation and earnings that have little to nothing to do with cash flow.

The first is unrealized derivative gains and losses. These gains and losses influence income but really not operations or cash flow.

The decline in depreciation may have something to do with the slowing growth more than anything else. Fast growth means more first year wells are in the depreciation component. Production declines rapidly but it declines from a high level compared to what comes later.

Depreciation does protect cash flow from taxation because it's a legitimate expense. But the decline shown above can come from the lack of recent growth combined with well improvements. That's not all the possibilities by any means. But these are likely the big reasons.

The bottom line is that the decline in EBITDAX shown above on the trend may not be all it seems to be. Cash flow is down with weaker commodity prices. That may be the decline that really matters.

Now EBITDAX is something that is often defined in mortgage agreements because it is non-GAAP. So, the trend can be concerning. On the other hand, management is likely to have taken this into account, and it may be covered by continuing well and operational improvements.

Cost Savings

The simulfrac savings likely affect depreciation going forward.

Notice also that not all wells will be completed this way. Now this savings will affect the average well cost that is depreciated. But it will take time for that savings to become significant enough to decrease depreciation.

Howard County is very much known for well profitability. Therefore, what's happening above is not unexpected. Now it represents a cost decrease because the more that gets produced for the same well cost, the smaller the per barrel costs. This may also help lease operating costs as well.

This is probably one of the major reasons that so many companies I follow are attracted to Howard County and surrounding areas.

What may happen as a result of this is a lower capital amount needed to maintain production. After that would be some slow growth while "living within means."

This will have to be followed up in the future to see if the actual realized area of results meets the initial expectations of management. Then we can tell if this is a potentially significant cost decrease as future production comes online.

Summary

The free cash flow situation that the market demands consistently appears to be getting a lot better than it was in the past during the period of fast growth. That's likely one of the key drivers behind the relaxed covenants for the debt.

Keep in mind that the company acquired the debt shown by getting to 50,000 BOED fast. So, this company began by wildly outspending cash flow in the hopes that production at some level would overtake that cash spending by generating more cash flow than needed for production.

At roughly 50,000 BOED, the company now maintains essentially a production level while improving costs and operations. What's shown below is much more recent examples of cost control due to concentrating on operations, well design, and any other improvements.

Now there's materially lower commodity prices that are offset by a lower capital budget, yet production really is not "budging" materially from the long-term goal. Keep in mind that commodity price declines come right off the bottom line with only a few deductions. That makes cost reductions a necessity in this industry. Otherwise, big losses happen very quickly. The comparison below demonstrates that with only a slight negative free cash flow due to commodity price declines.

The negative free cash flow is likely not at a level that would worry lenders. However, management does want to get to the point where the company shows free cash flow. That means that the considerable improvement shown still has a bit more to go.

The GAAP cash flow is clearly down, but it also clearly handles the debt load by most measures. Probably what will happen is if commodity prices continue to be soft, then there will be some informal pressure on the company to bring the debt down to keep the debt ratio closer to 1.0. No promises on that of course.

HighPeak Energy continues as a strong buy idea because it has that important Howard County (and nearby) location. Howard County is very much known for well profitability. This company has a lot of acreage that took some time to "get to know." But that means the high-grading process of prospects is likely becoming much more reliable (or reliable at all) than was the case in the past.

The company is now continuing to save costs where it can, which will likely maximize the stock price value. Jack Hightower, chairman and CEO, is a serial company starter whose experience likely puts this company far ahead of companies that are much larger. For that reason, I would likely consider holding this stock until he sells the company.

The worst is likely over for the stock price overhang relating to the debt issues. But that means that the current stock price likely has a lot of pessimism priced in which would limit downside risk from the current price.

That has to be taken in the context of the volatility of both relatively new issues (which this is) and the fact that the company is relatively small with a major shareholder (and hence management) controlling much of the issued stock. That means the float is a bit low for the stock that is issued.

Risks

A serial company starter like Hightower is a man of unusual talent whose services would be hard to replace if they were lost. Such a process is likely to materially (and likely adversely) affect the future of the company.

Note that Hightower has total control with that level of shares owned or controlled. He is therefore very material to the future of the company.

The current political uncertainty has resulted in weakening commodity prices combined with the industry largely "pulling back" to a more conservative stance. If the threats of trade wars and tariffs are not replaced with something constructive and permanent, the industry could materially shrink while likely facing a recession.

If commodity prices sustain a lower trajectory, it could materially change the outlook of the company.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Micron: The Underpriced AI Memory King Facing A Quiet Supply Shock
data.description:
Since Micron Technology (NASDAQ:MU) broke through the crucial $102.50 resistance and traded above the 2024 top price zone, it has been months later, and the market still does not understand the scale of the structural change taking place in this company. While investors focus on the surface narrative of AI and HBM (high-bandwidth memory) demand, the far more potent dynamic, the "trade ratio squeeze" creating artificial scarcity across the dynamic random-access memory, or DRAM, product space coupled with Micron's one-time geographic position outside of South Korea in a world of increasing geopolitical uncertainty, has seemingly been overlooked.

I feel that not only is Micron taking advantage of the AI upswing, but it is also facilitating a product mix transformation that will sustainably yield higher margins and growth in pricing power in the next 3-5 years. Micron is already secured for HBM production through 2025, with demand exceeding supply for 2026, they have already publicly indicated 10-15% price increases, and the same technical pattern indicates it can be much higher with upside potential of $156-190. Overall, Micron is a Strong Buy with a $160 price target within the next 12 months.

Business Update: The Numbers That Matter

Micron reported results for Q2 2025, and beat expectations for revenue and profit when they were announced on March 20, 2025. Revenue of $8.05 billion symbolized a 38.3% year over year increase in relation to Q2 2024 net income of $5.82 billion. Just as importantly, the adjusted earnings per share of $1.56 (adjusted), beat expectations by 9.1% and was a 271% year over year increase.

Segment results reveal the transformation that has taken place. Fiscal Q2 DRAM revenue reached $6.1 billion, up 47% year-over-year or 76% of total revenue. In $1 billion quarters, HBM revenue exceeded $1 billion, growing more than 50% sequentially. As Micron CEO Sanjay Mehrotra commented, "We were productively surprised that HBM shipments exceeded our plans, a clear reflection of our solid execution on the ongoing execution of the ramp."

The industry leading combination of high-capacity DRAM modules and LP DRAM revenue for the data center also went above the $1 billion milestone for the quarter. As Mehrotra reported, "Micron remains the only company in the world shipping low-power DRAM to the data centre in volume; we will continue our strong track record of leading edge innovation and solid deep partnership with our customers to develop differentiated solutions."

Micron's Q3 FY2025 results released on June 25 showed all-time revenue of $9.30 billion, exceeding its original $8.8 billion guidance by 6%. This compares to $8.05 billion in Q2 and $6.81 billion in the same quarter last year. Non‑GAAP EPS was $1.91 (well ahead of the prior quarter $1.56 and consensus estimates), GAAP EPS was $1.68. Gross margin increased to 39.0% on a non‑GAAP basis (was 37.9% Q2), while operating cash flow increased to $4.61 billion, up from $3.94 billion previous quarter.

Importantly for my thesis, DRAM revenue was at all-time highs and HBM revenue expanded nearly 50% Q on Q which validated the "trade ratio squeeze" dynamic I have outlined. Data center grew mo over mo and was over 2X YoY, confirming demand was AI driven. These results indicate pricing power and shift into HBM are translating into margin expansion and stronger cash flow generation.

The "Trade Ratio Squeeze": The Thesis That No One Is Seeing

While the market is zeroed in on the clear story of HBM growth, it is neglecting an even more powerful structural effect that I'm calling the "trade ratio squeeze". Whenever Micron is converting its production lines from conventional DRAM to HBM3E, it is not only benefitting from a higher margin associated with HBM, but it is also creating artificial scarcity vis à vis typical DRAM pricing across its portfolio.

HBM3E takes substantially more wafer area (per bit) than conventional DRAM, therefore, with each wafer dedicated to HBM, there are fewer DRAM bits available. This is the "trade ratio" dynamic that Micron mentioned in their earnings call: "We see the combined AI data center demand and the HBM ramp and its trade ratio creating both tension at the technology edge and a restriction on non-HBM DRAM supply."

This dynamic is already evident as price increases are becoming dramatic in size. Micron has officially announced overall price increases of 10-15% for DRAM and NAND products for 2025-2026. More dramatically, several DDR4 models report price increases in the range of 50% in a month, as stated by industry executives. Fixed transaction prices increased about 22% in April 2025 for general-purpose 8Gb DDR4 products. This pricing pressure is not transitory. The three manufacturers (Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron) have hastened DDR4 production cuts in order to allocate capacity toward DDR5 and higher margin products. Samsung is planned to terminate several DDR4 products utilizing a 1nm tapes, while SK Hynix plans to reduce DDR4's percentage of their total productions to 20%. The expectation is that DDR5's share of the market will surpass 80% by 2025 and it will supplant DDR4 as the market standard.

Competitive Analysis: The Hidden Geopolitical Advantage

The memory market has three powerful players, but geography also provides an underestimated context. For example, SK Hynix recently surpassed Samsung as the DRAM revenue leader for the first time since 1992 with 36% market share to Samsung's 34%. Micron is in third place with 25% share.

However, here is the important point being missed on Wall Street: about 75% of global DRAM production in geographically tied to South Korea. This concentration materialized as visible risk potential in December 2024 when South Korean President Yoon Suk-yeol declared martial law. The US markets experienced a lot of turbulence. However, hours later martial law was lifted, so the market calmed down quickly.

Micron is the only significant supply alternative outside of South Korea, and provides supply chain diversification on an axis that becomes more valuable as geopolitical tensions increase. For example, with the US restricting China's access to advanced semiconductors and China's response restricting core materials such as gallium and germanium, Micron's geographic context provides a supply stability which hyperscalers cannot ignore.

This trend is more pronounced in the crucial HBM space. In fact, in Q2 2025, SK Hynix dominated the market with a 62% share of HBM shipments, while Samsung struggled with a reduced market share of 17%, down from 41% last year. Micron has made significant gains as well, going from 4% to 21%. More importantly, Micron is a non-Korean supplier with meaningful HBM capacity, creating it as a critical strategic partner for supply diversification.

The Advantage of Energy Efficiency: More Than a Technical Feature

While competitors are locked in a battle over capacity, Micron has created a differentiating advantage that becomes more important every day: energy efficiency. Micron's HBM3E solutions use about 30% less power than other products in the market. In the power-hungry realm of the AI data center, this is not a trivial benefit. It is the single most important factor driving adoption.

AI data centers are using electricity at a rate about four times that of the new electricity arriving at the electrical infrastructure. The U.S. Department of Energy's Report for 2024 finds the U.S. data center industry currently consumes about 4.4% of all generated electricity, and is estimated to growth between 6.7% and 12% by 20278. Current state of the art GPU AI hardware operates in the range of 700 watts. It is estimated the next generation processor technologies in 2024 will be 1,200 watts.

Lower power usage is a direct contribution to TCO and lower operating costs. In addition, energy saved by using better memory can be made available for the deployment of more GPUs in the same power envelope to improve compute density and the performance of the data center. This generates switching costs and customer preference that exceed price.

Financial Analysis: Expanding Margins and Robust Cash Flow

Micron's transition to higher-margin products is creating remarkable financial results, and GAAP gross margin improved to 36.8% in Q2 2025 from 18.5% in the prior year period. More importantly, operating cash flow in Q2 was $3.94 billion compared to $3.24 billion in the prior quarter and $1.22 billion in the prior year period.

Entering Q2 with adjusted free cash flow at $857 million, Micron says it was able to convert profitability to cash, which is essential to fund the increased capital expenditures to sustain technological leadership. As noted, Micron has net capital expenditures in Q2 of $3.09 billion, meaning it is making extremely substantial investments for future capacity while adhering to capital discipline.

Operating margins are where the transformation story is most compelling. Micron's operating income in Q2 increased to $1.77 billion, or 22.0% to revenue versus only 3.3% a year ago. Margin expansion is a reflection of price recovery as well as an actual change in product mix to higher-value applications. The state of the balance sheet has also considerably strengthened. With cash, marketable investments, and restricted cash/assets at quarter-end, Micron has significant cash flow flexibility in placing itself to weather any potential market volatility. Lastly, with Micron's net debt-to-EBITDA multiple still at conservative levels, the company has flexibility in its balance sheet to make investments in growth or opportunistically acquire a business if it presents itself.

Valuation: Compressed Multiples Do Not Reflect Structural Transformation

Micron's stock is up 145%+ year to date and its valuation seems like it hasn't caught up to the structural change at the company to date. With a forward P/E of around 15x through 2026, it still trades through a steep discount versus its technology peers and its own margin improvement. My DCF model is based on conservative factors considering current visibility into the business.

Assumptions base case:

  • Revenue Growth: (+) 25% CAGR 2025-27 (assumed ramp in HBM and improvement in DRAM price)
  • Operating Margin: 28% by 2026 (22% currently to 28% progressive improvement)
  • WACC: 9.8% (Risk-free rate 4.3% + equity risk premium 5.5%)
  • Terminal growth: 3% (long-run nominal GDP)
  • HBM market share: 23% at year-end 2025 (management guidance)

Valuation Sensitivity Analysis:

  • Bull Case ($190): HBM reaches a $10 billion run rate by year-end 2025, 25% share of the market, and 32% operating margins
  • Base Case ($160): Deliver according to guidance, Margins stabilize at 28%, moderate growth for HBM
  • Bear Case ($85): An unexpected slowdown in AI demand, DRAM price war, margins compress to 18%

The valuation relative to peer multiples also indicates further upside. Advanced Micro Devices trades at 28x forward earnings, NVIDIA at 35x, and Micron trades at just 15x despite experiencing faster earnings growth. Even applying a conservative 20x to my 2026 EPS forecast of $8.00 would yield a valuation of $160 fair value.

Beyond my DCF work and peer multiple cross‑check, this view is further corroborated by Seeking Alpha Quant’s Factor Grades, which currently rate Micron a Strong Buy with a very balanced profile: Valuation at B, Growth at A+, Profitability at A+, Momentum at B+, and Revisions at A− — all of which have improved steadily over the past 3–6 months as the shift toward HBM has shown up in accelerating revenue and expanding margins. In practical terms, this aligns with my bridge to 2026: a forward P/E in the mid‑teens still screens at a discount to peers despite faster earnings growth, while consensus estimate revisions turned decisively positive after the Q2 beat/raise and the Q3 record revenue of $9.30B with nearly 50% sequential HBM growth.

I treat these grades as secondary corroboration — not a primary driver — but they track closely with the three core engines of the thesis: (1) market share gains and higher ASPs in HBM, (2) DRAM supply side tightening via trade ratio effect, and, (3) measurable energy efficiency advantage that translates to customer preference and pricing power, all of which are already evident in margins, free cash flow, and the apparent bullish skew in analyst revisions.

Catalysts for the Next 6-12 Months

There are several specific catalysts lined up to support stock performance over the next twelve months. The Q3 FY2025 figures, which I previously provided on June 25th, showed record revenues, which also validated my thesis: HBM revenue +50% QoQ, record DRAM sales, strong data center growth, and margin expansion above Q2.

The ramp of 1-gamma DRAM production is another significant catalyst. Micron is extending its technology leadership with the introduction of its 1-gamma node and also as the company moved into production on the industry's first shipments of 1-gamma based DDR5 products last month. This technology drills down to EUV lithography and has density advantages that translate into cost advantages and higher margins for Micron when compared to other products.

Guidance updates for fiscal year 2026 will be another significant catalyst. The HBM business is on a trajectory to a $10 billion run rate by calendar year-end 2025 and a target market share of 22-23%. However, I believe management will give optimistic guidance that surprises the market positively.

The product roadmap also offers some specific enablers. HBM4 samples are expected to be mid-2025, volume production is expected in 2026. HBM4 promises 2TB/s of bandwidth with 20% less power consumption, indicating that Micron is well position to maintain competitive advantages in the next generation of AI accelerators.

Technical Analysis: Confirming the Fundamental Thesis

The technical analysis that you provide gives solid backing to the bullish fundamental thesis above. The chart unmistakably establishes that as of today's date Micron is contained within a long-term ascending wedge that dates back to the 2008 crash resulting from the subprime crisis. It's amazing there has been a 9,807% advance from the bottom of $1.59 to the first high of $157.54 June 18, 2024 report.

The false breakout under the wedge (denoted in the gray circle) is indicative of extremely bullish technical structure. The false breakout, then swift recovery is a strong indication institutional buyers are very aggressively defending the support of the wedge and pursuing any opportunities.

The gap up last week of 2.52% after confirming the breakout of the resistance at $102.5 indicates that institutional accumulation is massive. Now, the stock needs to confront the bearish trendline from the last year, and if it can sustain a breakout of this downward trendline, it will have clear sailing to the prior highs of $156, and potentially even to the top of the wedge of around $190.

The 50-, 100-, and 200-day moving averages all are pointing up on the weekly chart and confirm a solid medium-term uptrend. The stochastic is also in the overbought territory up to 91.76, but given how powerful the fundamental momentum is, I suspect the stock will continue to be strong and upward before any significant consolidation.

The key level to watch is $130, because if Micron cannot convincingly break and hold that level, I think the risk of a double bottom back toward the $70-$80 zone is a real risk. However, considering all the fundamental catalysts and defensive market action that has incorrectively broken, I consider this bearish scenario as low probability.

Specific Risks That Could Invalidate the Thesis

There are three specific risks that could undermine my bullish thesis and require me to reconsider the position. The first risk is an unexpected slowdown in AI-related data center CapEX spending. If hyperscalers stop aggressive expansions because the ROI is not there or due to regulatory constraints, demand for HBM could slide much lower than I expect. I will continue to track quarterly CapEX announcements by Microsoft, Google, Amazon, and Meta, all of which will be leading indicators.

The second risk is a new intensification of geopolitical conflict causing supply chain disruption in the world. Micron is based outside of South Korea and diversifies its supply chain, meaning that it is less reliant on suppliers in Asia for chip production equipment and raw materials, but Micron is still reliant on Asian suppliers for the core materials and equipment. New tensions between the United States and China, or new geopolitical conflict in the Taiwan Strait could lead to disruptions in the overall semiconductor operating environment.

The third risk is the risk of unforeseen technology competition to Micron’s HBM advantages. While Micron maintains its lead in efficiency and performance with products, both Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK Hynix (traded in South Korea) are pouring billions into research and development. A technology that would match or exceed Micron performance would cut into our market share and pricing power.

To watch for these risks, I will monitor three indicators: data center capacity utilization, competitive company new product announcements, and management comments regarding pricing and demand during quarterly earnings calls. A decline of 15% from $102.50 would change my originally bullish thesis.

Conclusion

Micron Technology Inc. offers a transformational investment opportunity that the market is mispricing. While the company will benefit from the AI boom through its position in HBM leadership, it is also enacting a fundamental transformation of its product mix that will provide higher margins and pricing power for the foreseeable future.

I believe the "trade ratio squeeze" dynamic I am identifying will create artificial shortages in conventional DRAM, as HBM demand accelerates.

Not only does Micron's 30% energy efficiency advantage become critical as data centers deal with power constraints, its geographic position out of South Korea gives its enterprise customers supply chain diversification neither company can ignore.

With HBM production sold through 2025, price increases of 10-15% already occurring, and numerous catalysts bouncing around over the next twelve months, I am maintaining my Strong Buy rating and $160 price target which represents 25% upside from current prices. I am confident in my thesis based on a combination of solid fundamentals and technical confirmation, which makes Micron a core holding for all AI revolution-focused portfolios.

data.title: Markets Weekly Outlook: Jackson Hole, NZ Rate Decisions And U.K./EU Inflation Data
data.description:
By Elior Manier

After an already volatile trading week, next week will see a crossroads of data and geopolitical catalysts that may move currency, equity, and crypto markets.

Before looking at those, let's take a look at what happened this week.

Week in review: US mixed inflation data sending warning signs, RBA rate cut, and Trump-Putin meeting

The US path to a much anticipated rate cut was well-drawn, particularly after Tuesday's CPI report coming along the Fed's inflation target (2.5% y/y on the headline, 2.7% on the core.)

However, Thursday's PPI data changed the narrative quite a lot. Coming in at 0.9% vs. 0.2% expected (and bringing the y/y core to 3.7%!), markets caught a bad surprise: tariff-led inflation is making its way to the US data.

With a mix of downward revised NFP data and a not-so-clear US inflation outlook, the September rate cut isn't going to be such a given. There will still be one more NFP report and CPI report before the 17th of September meeting happens.

In the meantime, risk assets and sentiment have taken a hit from the tariff fears: cryptos have taken a tough hit, and US indices are a bit more indecisive yet still holding close to their recent highs.

Weekly performance from different asset classes

The Trump-Putin meeting should also be making headlines very soon, with the two presidents starting discussions in the afternoon. The Kremlin spokesperson, Dmitry Peskov, expects a long meeting, saying that "In general, we can imagine that it will take at least 6-7 hours."

Access our WTI Oil analysis as some heightened volatility can be expected there in the afternoon, with volatility in the commodity possibly also spilling on Monday. Elsewhere, markets saw the Royal Bank of Australia cutting rates to 3.60% on mixed signals for the future RBA outlook.

The Week Ahead: Jackson Hole Symposium and a lot more

One of the key events concerning all types of markets is the yearly Jackson Hole Symposium. A roadmap for the event just got published. For those who don't know, the Jackson Hole Economic Policy Symposium is an annual gathering hosted by the Kansas City Fed, bringing together central bankers, policymakers, academics, and market participants to discuss key economic issues.

While the topics vary each year, it's closely watched for views from central banks on the future outlook with tariff concerns and global growth.

One of the most memorable market-shaking moments from the yearly conference came in 2010, when then-Fed Chair Ben Bernanke used his Jackson Hole speech to signal a second round of quantitative easing (QE2).

The hint sent risk assets surging and Treasury yields lower, cementing Jackson Hole's reputation as a stage for major policy signals.

Asia Pacific Markets: Royal Bank of New Zealand rate decision

Asian-Pacific markets will not get too much in terms of key data, with the main event happening in New Zealand. The RBNZ is widely expected to cut their rates to 3% from 3.25%. Keep an eye on our upcoming RBNZ meeting preview coming up on Monday. The rate decision is happening on Tuesday at 22:00 ET.

For the rest, mid-tier data releases include Japan and New Zealand's trade balance, the Japanese national CPI data, and Australian PMIs. Markets are also awaiting the PBoC rate decision, just before the RBNZ meeting.

The Chinese economy has been stagnant for a while now, except for the stimulus offered by the central bank. Economists are waiting to see if there is more stimulus to come to boost notably APAC currencies (like AUD and NZD) and commodities' growth outlook.

US, Europe, and UK markets: PMI data, Canadian inflation, Eurozone, and UK PPI releases

The week really starts on Tuesday with the Canadian inflation forecasted at 2%. We will see if the Loonie gets enough of a push to strengthen after a rough past week. The CAD has been getting dragged around by US dollar flows, with the USD/CAD stuck in consolidation.

Markets will also see if the UK inflation gets a boost, which may confirm further the doubts of prolonged rate cuts from the Bank of England. The BoE conference on Thursday, August 7th, had almost failed to deliver a cut.

In Europe, both the UK and Germany will release their PPI data on Wednesday, where we will see how producer prices moved on the other side of the Atlantic. They might be movers for European currencies after this week's market shaking US PPI.

The US doesn't have many economic releases per se, but will still see a few events, including the FOMC Minutes on Wednesday (14:00) and many key speeches from a divided Federal Reserve, including Jerome Powell in Jackson Hole scheduled at 10:00 AM on Friday.

Of course, don't forget to check all the different PMI releases expected from the Eurozone, the UK, and finally the US (Thursday 9:45 for US global services PMI).

Safe trades and enjoy your weekend!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SoundHound: Likely To Take Off After Raising FY 2025 Guidance
data.description:
The last time I covered SoundHound AI, Inc. (NASDAQ:SOUN) was back in March, during the market meltdown. Naturally, I rated this stock as a strong sell, as fundamentals and valuation suddenly begin to matter more than the narrative or the growth when the broader markets are contracting. In these times, funds tend to derisk their portfolios, and more often than not, stocks with sky-high valuations tend to be the first ones to go.

With the markets back at all-time highs, I considered reviewing speculative, high-growth stocks, like SoundHound. I admit that I was quite surprised after the company crushed Q2 earnings expectations and increased its FY 2025 revenue guidance. At the mid-point of that guidance ($167 million), the company is essentially targeting a triple-digit growth this year (well, a 98.8% to be specific).

The growth story, especially in the QSR segment, remains strong. Margins improved sequentially to 58% non-GAAP (39% GAAP), and the CFO is reviewing low margin contracts. Speaking of which, there is a risk that could make near-term revenue a bit lumpy, as some restaurant deals renew and volume discounts may no longer be available.

Overall, I see upside ahead, mainly in the QSR segment, with an attractive price action following a breakout after Q2 earnings.

Why I Wasn't Wrong With My Strong Sell Rating

On March 13, 2025, my article titled "SoundHound: Spectacular Q4 Earnings Can't Drown Out The Broader Market Selloff" was published on SA.

If you have been reading my previous articles, you know that I pay little to no attention to valuation analysis for most (but not all) stocks when the broader markets are expanding. Notice this is not the case during a broader market contraction or stagnation. Why?

Excess liquidity. I won't go into the details here, but I call excess liquidity when there’s more cash or credit looking for a home than the real economy or safe assets can easily absorb at current rates, so it spills into risk assets like equities. The opposite is also true: when the broader markets are selling off due to deteriorating economics or the fear in anticipation of a deteriorating economic landscape, market participants typically deleverage and unload the most speculative assets in their portfolios (I often inverse my long positions and go short).

Well, that's exactly what happened in Q1 and part of Q2 this year. In December last year and January this year, I was bullish on SoundHound, as I hadn’t seen any signs of deterioration in the US economy. Therefore, its customer growth, particularly in the QSR segment, along with the triple-digit revenue growth, caught my attention, even if the company was burning cash and had a stretched valuation.

A quick side note: it is my strong belief as an active market participant that in times of an expansionary economy, most stocks that have an attractive growth story and/or fundamentals thrive, regardless (up to a point, of course) of poor valuation or cash burn. For me, if a company doesn’t have any major drags (think Intel’s Foundry segment) weighing on sentiment, and it can grow revenue consistently in the double digits (SoundHound has been growing revenue in the triple digits since Q4 last year), it's generally well-positioned. Of course, there are more angles to my analysis, but that’s the 20% that drives 80% of my rationale (think Pareto).

Then, Trump began teasing his reciprocal tariff agenda, and I turned bearish on most risk assets (i.e., US public equities). SoundHound was one of them, trading at a TTM P/S ratio of 39 and a TTM P/B of 21 when I rated it a strong sell.

Was I right with my rating? In my view, the answer is yes, even if it didn't hit my target (high $3s). Let me explain.

Between March 13 and April 7, the stock plunged an eye watering 28%. If that drop is not enough for a strong sell rating, you tell me what is in the comment section.

Do you understand now why I run away from high beta stocks during a selloff? That's why I said in my March coverage:

I anticipate the SPY will rock bottom at around $480, with speculative AI stocks like SoundHound likely experiencing steeper losses due to their high beta.

Of course, with the pause of reciprocal tariffs on April 9, the economic landscape suddenly improved, businesses gained some certainty around trade policies, and pretty much every US equity regained its momentum prior to the tariff-driven selloff.

So, why didn't you review your rating? (some readers may rightfully say)

I’ll be as honest, transparent, and direct as I can: the day only has so many hours, and even if I wished I could stop time, its passage is inevitable. I don't have a team of analysts to help me cover all the stocks in my universe. Therefore, I need to be hyper-selective with the stocks that I analyze based on the macro and industry trends. After the April 9 selloff, my attention was mostly focused on semiconductor stocks and mega caps. SoundHound didn't really emerge on my radar until now.

Would I have reconsidered my rating on SoundHound on April 9? Unequivocally, yes. That’s water under the bridge. Let’s focus on the present.

An AI Growth Stock

Q2 2025 revenue came in at $42.7 million, up 217% from a year ago, making it the all-time strongest quarter for the company, with growth in all key business units.

As seen above, the segment that contributes the most to revenue is hosted services, with +140% yoy growth in Q2.

Furthermore, the company raised its revenue outlook for 2025, now projecting $160 million to $178 million in revenue. Furthermore, they expect revenue to ramp up as the year exits, targeting adjusted EBITDA profitability exiting 2025.

All that said, we believe we can achieve adjusted EBITDA profitability as we exit 2025. - CFO Nitesh Sharan, Q2 2025 earnings call

In terms of margins, the company achieved a GAAP gross margin of 39% and a non-GAAP gross margin of 58% in Q2, both up sequentially. However, as seen below, the trend is anything but encouraging.

The CFO expects to realize more cost synergies, including cloud synergies, in the coming quarters, where he also noted a decline in like-for-like inference costs. Most importantly, he is evaluating low-margin contracts.

And we are still evaluating low margin contracts as they come up for renewal and identify other areas to improve efficiency.

I believe there could be a major risk here. According to the statement above, those low margin contracts (I believe they have volume-based discounts) are in for a renewal. I cannot help but wonder if their customers in the restaurant segment (a very low margin business) signed up mainly due to these discounts. If they have to pay full price, I believe we could see some revenue lumpiness ahead. In fact, the CFO already hinted at some volatility ahead:

While we hope to repeat that each and every quarter, sometimes the lumpy nature of these deals can cause quarter-to-quarter volatility.

As for customer concentration risk, no customer accounted for more than 10% of the total revenue through the first half of 2025. The list of customers is quite impressive. From what I read in the earnings call, new wins in Q2 include IHOP, Red Lobster, and Peter Piper Pizza. Also, MOD Pizza renewed its contract, Red Robin added Smart Answering, and there were expansions at Chipotle, as well as further rollouts at Firehouse Subs, Habit Burger, McAlister’s Deli, Beef O’Brady’s, and Casey’s. SoundHound is scaling its operations, with an active network of over 14,000 restaurants, 1,000 new locations, and approximately 1M new interactions in Q2.

I have to say that I like the growth in the QSR business, but I would like to see a revenue breakdown per business type (i.e., restaurant vs non-restaurant) to gauge which of the segments has better traction. Unfortunately, it's too early for SoundHound to reveal those figures (as competitors could focus on the higher growth segments and disregard the others).

Speaking of other business units, the drive-thru business is picking up steam, with hundreds of live sites and a strong ecosystem of partners (i.e., Par, Samsung, and HME). Furthermore, the company is mitigating the 6 to 12-month retrofit delay for customers by offering smaller footprint posts (i.e., tablet + mic) in the drive-thru lanes. The results show that average order times are coming down from a minute plus to less than a minute.

In Retail, the company has deals with two multi-hundred unit personal-care companies and a home services company with more than 500 locations. As for the financial services vertical, 7 of the top 10 global FIs are customers, and 4 of them have renewed or were upsold. Furthermore, the automated conversation volume in this sector is doubling year over year, with expansion across regional banks and credit unions.

Moving on to the automotive segment, the company has launched its Chat AI solution in North America, along with three additional global automotive brands. Additionally, they secured several new deals, including a large-scale program in China, representing "millions of units".

Interestingly enough, it seems that ASPs (and therefore, ARPUs, average revenue per user) are growing with the new gen AI features deployed:

In automotive, we signed a new OEM in China and saw a double-digit increase in ASPs primarily from further expansion of our Generative AI solutions.

Finally, from a cash perspective, SoundHound ended the quarter with $230 million in cash and cash equivalents and no debt. As seen below, the cash burn in the last 12 months has surpassed $110 million.

Therefore, SoundHound has a cash runway of little over two years. Unless I see shares trading back in the mid-$20s, I wouldn't worry too much about dilution. That said, there are big risks to consider with this stock.

Risks

As I noted before, revenue may be a little volatile as lower-margin contracts are up for renewal. I believe SoundHound will either completely eliminate these volume discounts or reduce them drastically. Considering the low margins of QSR restaurants (see below Red Robin's EBITDA margin), I believe some may not renew contracts if discounts are completely eliminated.

In fact, churn was one of the topics discussed during the last earnings call:

where there maybe previously had been leakage and some churn that we're refining that we're turning that around into -- from reductions into maintenance to actually growth.

I recommend keeping a close eye on this topic, especially as lower margin contracts are due for renewal (possibly without volume based discounts). The CFO emphasized in the last earnings call that momentum won't be linear.

We have said before that our momentum won't be linear, and we are moving fast to capture the underlying market shifts, which are clearly in our favor.

Speaking of momentum in revenue, it seems that growth this year will be backloaded to Q4 rather than Q3 due to seasonality.

Other risks come from a significant increase in stock based compensation. Over the past 12 months, the company SBC accounted for $60.2 million. That's 82% more than in FY 2024. I am not particularly encouraged about the recent increase; however, the company is growing revenue in the triple digits, so for now, I see this hike in SBC as partially justified.

Valuation

Considering that SoundHound is in the early stages of its growth phase, we have just a few valuation ratios to look at. On a P/S basis, the company is trading at 40x next year's earnings. That is quite a premium when compared to the sector median of just 3.17x.

On a P/E basis, I believe it's too early to talk about profitability. Even the Street is not forecasting a positive EPS over the next couple of years.

Therefore, this is an expensive stock, and value investors should consider this as a risk.

Looking Ahead

SoundHound surprised the revenue consensus in the past quarter by about $9.8 million more than initially expected, but most importantly, it raised FY 2025 guidance. At the mid-point ($167 million), we are talking about a 98.8% yoy increase, almost in the triple digits.

The growth is undeniable, and SoundHound is one of the few players that trade publicly in the US, offering AI services. We are in the middle of an AI supercycle, which I believe could well last until the end of the decade. This, in addition to favorable price action after the recent breakout post earnings, makes me rate SoundHound as a speculative buy. I would keep an eye on customer renewals, particularly in the QSR segment, as those low-margin contracts come to an end.

data.title: Claritev's Stock Performance Could Be Shooting Past Q2 Performance: I Choose A Hold Rating
data.description:
Claritev (NYSE:CTEV) released its Q2 2O25 numbers on August 6 and since then the stock's price which has been on an upward trend has even been more bullish since the latest financials. In just under two weeks, the stock has surged by over 35% and in my view this performance is almost scary. Yes, Q2 has been a good quarter for the company but given the other risks facing the group, now including negative equity, I don't think the level at which the stock price is trading at will be sustainable in the near term. I wouldn't recommend investors to keep accumulating CTEV stock. The best I would do in my view is to HOLD at this point.

What Q2 Performance Looked Like

The provider of healthcare tech-enabled solutions saw revenue rising 3.5% year-on-year (YoY) to $241.6 million whilst EBITDA margins expanded to 63.8%. The revenue surge was also higher than last quarter's level at $231.3 million. That said, it is important to take note that the quarterly revenue figure was also buoyed by a $5 million non-recurring revenue from a new commercial arrangement in its Property & Casualty (P&C) business.

What that means is that that additional revenue does not represent a sustainable recurring revenue. Management also highlighted that it expects a total of roughly $15 million in similar one-off P&C revenue for FY25, with comparable contributions expected in Q3 and Q4.

Besides the $5 million P&C figure, Claritev still managed to record revenue gains for both YoY and from previous quarter. I think that top line performance is quite commendable for a year dubbed as the 'Year of the Turn'.

Positives to Support 'Year of the Turn' Narrative

Claritev has had a successful Q2 after attaining strong client retention and expansion wins. The company managed to renew a top five client and a top 20 client early. Both retentions are for five-year terms.

On top of that, the company managed to close an $81 million+ 10-year subscription deal for Ben Insights. Ben Insights is one of Claritev’s data and decision science products. This deal is the largest in the company's history and carries additional upside potential of $20 million+ according to the earnings call. Meanwhile, this deal is expected to start generating meaningful recurring software subscription revenue in 2026.

In my view, the magnitude of this win must not be underestimated especially in light of how other sectoral peers are faring. American Well Corporation (NYSE:AMWL), or Amwell, a SaaS-based technology-enabled healthcare platform provider, during the same period had its U.S. Defense Health Agency (DHA), a division of the U.S. Department of Defense (DoD) that manages the Military Health System (MHS), contract being extended by only a year instead of the expected three years plus a nine-month transition.

Claritev's wins are more pronounced with renewals at five years and 10-years+. That's quite impressive, isn't it?

In addition, the company also signed new logos across multiple market segments during the second quarter. These included five in the payer/Third Party Administrator (TPA) space and three in the provider market. Meanwhile, partnerships with Oracle, Athena, and Burjeel are still producing minimal income. What that implies is that execution will be the critical hurdle for CTEV, I think.

Overall, there's a good story to tell as far as Claritev's operations are concerned.

Insider Trading

CTEV's top management continued with more purchases of its own stock.

In May, purchases by the top three executives amounted to $1.18 million, led by CFO Garis Douglas Michael, COO Hogge Jerome, and CEO Travis Dalton. The stock price at that time was averaging around $25.

That said, between August 7-11, further purchases by Travis and Jerome amounting to a total of $549 million is a sign of confidence in the company on the part of top leadership. Most noteworthy, is the stock prices at which these latest August purchases took place at which were averaging around $55. Top executives were and are willing to trade CTEV stock at a premium of over 120% from that price of three-months ago. They should be seeing something great in their operations, right?

Leverage Plus Negative Equity: CTEV's Major Headache

Despite all the positives, the company's debt load and high interest burden remain heavy overhangs. Shareholder equity has been on a sustained decline and for the past seven consecutive quarters (the ones under review below, could be longer) highlighted, the current quarter is the first for the group to have a negative figure at -$39.9 million.
